macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Daf and Core Halachic Questions About Yayin Nesech and Stam Yeinam

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on the daf's length and the distinction between Yayin Nesech (Biblical) and Stam Yeinam (Rabbinic)
Core question: Does the prohibition of benefit (issur hana'ah) apply to Stam Yeinam as it does to actual Yayin Nesech?
Introduction to major disputes among Rishonim on the status of Stam Yeinam and Rabbinic safeguards

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Non-Jew Climbing the Palm Tree and Rav's Ruling on Wine Touched Unintentionally

micro_segments.title: The incident: A non-Jew climbing a date palm, using a lulav branch to harvest, and inadvertently touching a flask of wine without intention (shelo b'kavana)
Rav's initial ruling: permissible to sell to non-Jews (sheriya Rav lizevunei le'ovdei kochavim)
Challenge from Rav Kahana and Rav Assi: citing Rav's own teaching that even a day-old baby makes yayin nesech
Rav's defense: distinguishing between drinking (asur) and benefit/sale (mutar), and between children and unintentional contact

macro_segments.title: Rav's Teaching on Children and the Braita Distinguishing Adults from Minors Regarding Yayin Nesech

micro_segments.title: Rav restates his position: tinok ben yomo oseh yayin nesech (a day-old baby creates yayin nesech)
Challenge from Rav Shimi bar Hiyya via a Tosefta braita distinguishing adults (who know idolatry) from children (who do not)

macro_segments.title: The Broader Braita on Slave Acquisition and Conversion: Tumah (Impurity) and Wine Status Across Ages and Conversion Stages

micro_segments.title: Full Tosefta braita: purchases of slaves, requirements of milah and tvila; children born to slave women (benei shfachot)
Tumah laws for unconverted/partially converted slaves: spit and midras in public; versions reading tamei vs. tahor
Wine section of braita: adults (malu velo tavlu) make yayin nesech; children do not; distinction based on knowledge of idolatry
Alternative interpretation: braita implies only adults create yayin nesech, contradicting Rav's position on children

macro_segments.title: Rav's Teaching on Children and the Braita Distinguishing Adults from Minors Regarding Yayin Nesech (continued)

micro_segments.title: Analysis of the braita's reasoning: adults know idolatry practices; children do not, hence no yayin nesech from children
Rav's interpretation: the braita refers specifically to benei shfachot (children of slave women), not all children

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's More Stringent Position: Even After Full Conversion (Malo v'Tavlo), Wine Remains Problematic Until Idolatry is Renounced

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's teaching via Rav Nachman: purchased slaves, even after milah and tvila, create yayin nesech until they renounce idolatry
Reason: even formal conversion doesn't eliminate internal religious beliefs; must wait until idolatry is forgotten/renounced
Discussion of the realities of slave conversion, religious attachment, and the practical difficulty of assessing personal belief
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's quantification: twelve months of living in a Jewish home suffices for renunciation

macro_segments.title: Rava's Challenge to Shmuel via the Same Braita: Attempted Proof That Age, Not Full Renunciation, Determines Status

micro_segments.title: Rava cites the braita again, arguing it proves that after malo v'tavlo, even slaves no longer create yayin nesech
Shmuel's counterinterpretation: the braita speaks only of benei shfachot (who grew up Jewish), not purchased adult slaves
Reconciliation and nuance: both agree on the core principle but focus on different circumstances; main disagreement is about children
Conclusion: Rav thinks children create yayin nesech; Shmuel thinks they do not; braita proves each position from opposite angles

macro_segments.title: The Case in Mahoza: Non-Jew Enters a Wine Shop, Touches Wine in a Bucket, and Wine Is Mixed Into a Barrel

micro_segments.title: Narrative: non-Jew enters Jewish wine merchant's shop, asks for wine, merchant says no; non-Jew puts hand in bucket and stirs
Outcome: non-Jew questions if it's wine; merchant sells it to him (perhaps reluctantly); merchant then pours this wine into a large barrel in anger
Rava permits selling the diluted barrel to non-Jews (but not clear if drinking is permitted); question of bitul (nullification) and hana'ah
Two interpretations of Rava's decision: either stam yeinam is generally mutar b'hana'ah, or bitul applies here due to mixture/unintentional blending

macro_segments.title: Public Dispute Over Rava's Lenient Ruling: Rav Huna's Opposition and Competing Proclamations in Mahoza

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna bar Chinana and Rav Huna, son of Rav Nachman, disagree with Rava; each rabbi sends out students to proclaim their position
Rava instructs his attendant to bar Rav Huna from entering Mahoza due to his stringency causing disturbance
Rav Huna arrives in Mahoza anyway to confront Rava; questions why he permitted the wine case given his own prior teaching
Rava, under pressure, modifies his position: the wine should be prohibited even in benefit (asur afilu b'hana'ah), except for the value of the non-kosher portion
Rava appeals to being challenged by Abaye at Pumbedita with sources and mishna ruling the whole barrel prohibited; transition to next sugya

topical_tags.term: Malou v'tavlo / Malo v'tavlo (underwent circumcision and immersion)
Knowledge of idolatry practices (da'at avodah zarah)
Shmuel's position: renunciation of idolatry (ad shetishka avodah zarah mipiehem)
Rav Huna bar Chinana and Rav Huna son of Rav Nachman (stringent opposition)
Safek derabbanan lekula (doubt in Rabbinic law is lenient)
Tosefta braita on slaves and conversion
